label: Genres
description: The stylistic vibe or market category the book belongs to; the emotional promise made to the reader.

tags.definition: Works in which reason and logic are systematically undermined; the universe is indifferent or irrational
Plot-driven works where physical conflict, speed, and kinetic energy are the primary draw
Works centered on a journey, quest, or exploration into unfamiliar or dangerous territory
Works whose primary intent is to amuse; structured around comic situations, misunderstandings, or wit
Works in which criminal activity (planning, execution, or consequence) is the central subject
Works that explore serious human situations and emotional conflict without a dominant genre framework
Works in which explicit sexual content is a primary and intentional element
Works set in worlds with magic, mythological creatures, or supernatural systems that operate by internal rules
Works set substantially in a documented past era, where that era's conditions shape the story
Works whose primary intent is to produce fear, dread, or revulsion in the reader
Works that use comedy as a stylistic mode rather than a structural one; lighter than Comedy
Works in which queer identity, relationships, or experience is a central (not incidental) element
Works where language, form, and prose style are foregrounded as primary artistic concerns
Works structured around the retrospective discovery of the truth about a hidden event (typically a crime)
Works drawing substantially from or retelling mythological traditions
Works in which a central love relationship and its emotional development drives the plot
Works that use irony, exaggeration, or ridicule to critique society, institutions, or human nature
Works that extrapolate from scientific or technological possibility into speculative scenarios
Works structured around tension, urgency, and the forward-looking prevention of catastrophe
Works in which the protagonist's downfall through flaw, circumstance, or fate is the central arc
Works set in the American West (typically 19th century) and engaging its themes of frontier, law, and survival
